agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled, how invoicing works, and when invoicing occurs in the order lifecycle.","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial commitment between the business and a customer, progressing through a lifecycle from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing the business commitment. Subject to lifecycle constraints."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Per finance, invoicing is irreversible once applied to an order."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The moment an order is created and accepted into the system. Per operations, this is the point at which invoicing occurs."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The act of dispatching goods to the customer. Per the PM, the cutoff point after which cancellation is no longer permitted."},{"term":"Invoiced (state)","definition":"An order that has had an invoice generated against it. Per finance, this state is permanent and prevents cancellation."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business policy from the PM defining the cancellation window. A VP could change this policy (e.g., narrow the window).","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, a cancellation request succeeds."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled; invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Invoicing irreversibility is a financial integrity constraint. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders would corrupt financial records, violate accounting standards, and create reconciliation failures.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in the Invoiced state, a cancellation request is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy choice about when to invoice. A VP could decide to invoice at a different lifecycle stage (e.g., at shipment).","testable_assertion":"Given a newly placed order, an invoice is generated immediately and the order transitions to Invoiced state."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Customer places an order and immediately requests cancellation","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — BR-1 says this should succeed (order has not shipped), but BR-2 + BR-3 together mean the order is already invoiced at placement and therefore uncancellable."},{"scenario":"Order placed but shipment delayed for weeks","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — PM expects cancellation is available during this entire window, but finance and operations rules make it impossible from the moment of placement."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment scenario","expected_behavior":"UNDEFINED — If an order is partially shipped, it is unclear whether the unshipped portion can be cancelled, and the invoice already covers the full order."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"If BR-2+BR-3 prevail, the business cannot cancel any order after placement, which may increase refund/chargeback costs and damage customer trust. If BR-1 prevails, the business must solve invoice reversal (credit notes, voids), which has accounting and audit implications.","operational":"Operations invoices at placement to lock pricing — changing this timing would require reworking the pricing guarantee mechanism. Allowing cancellation of invoiced orders would require a credit note or reversal workflow that does not currently exist.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (BR-1). If this is impossible due to immediate invoicing, customer satisfaction and retention will suffer. This is a direct conflict between internal financial controls and customer-facing policy.","risk":"HIGH — Implementing any interpretation without resolving this contradiction will either (a) violate financial integrity by cancelling invoiced orders, (b) violate customer expectations by blocking all cancellations, or (c) introduce undefined behavior. Legal/regulatory risk exists if invoicing rules violate local accounting standards.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders at all after placement if finance rules prevail, contradicting the stated cancellation policy."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If cancellation of invoiced orders is allowed, financial records become inconsistent. Credit note/void workflows would need to be designed."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is moved away from placement, the pricing lock-in mechanism must be redesigned."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the frontline burden of explaining why cancellations are rejected despite published policy."},{"group":"Product Management","impact":"The stated cancellation policy is unimplementable under current finance and operations constraints."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The three requirements contain a direct logical contradiction. BR-1 permits cancellation before shipment. BR-3 invoices at placement. BR-2 prohibits cancellation of invoiced orders. Combining BR-2 and BR-3 means no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting BR-1. No amount of analysis can resolve this without a stakeholder decision. Confidence is ≤50 per the contradiction stop condition.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and unambiguous — it is a logical impossibility, not a matter of interpretation","BR-2 (invoice irreversibility) is a genuine financial integrity invariant","The three rules, taken together, make cancellation impossible despite BR-1 explicitly allowing it"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which rule should yield — this requires a business decision, not a technical one","Whether a credit note / void mechanism is an acceptable resolution","Whether 'invoiced' and 'cancellable' can coexist via a compensating transaction pattern"],"assumptions":["All three statements are accurately representing current stakeholder intent","There is no existing credit note or invoice void mechanism that would reconcile these rules","'Invoicing' means the same thing to both finance and operations teams"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The three requirements form a logical impossibility. BR-1 says orders can be cancelled before shipment. BR-3 says orders are invoiced at placement. BR-2 says invoiced orders cannot be cancelled. Therefore: every order is invoiced the moment it is placed, and no invoiced order can be cancelled, so no order can ever be cancelled — directly contradicting BR-1. These three rules cannot all be true simultaneously.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Customer Support"],"options":["(A) Finance yields: Introduce a credit note / invoice void mechanism so that invoiced orders CAN be cancelled before shipment. Invoicing remains at placement for pricing lock-in, but cancellation triggers a compensating financial transaction rather than being blocked.","(B) Operations yields: Move invoicing from placement to a later lifecycle stage (e.g., shipment or pick-pack). Find an alternative mechanism to lock in pricing (e.g., a price snapshot on the order). This preserves both the cancellation window and invoice irreversibility.","(C) Product yields: Accept that orders cannot be cancelled after placement. Change the customer-facing policy to reflect this reality. Potentially offer a return/refund flow instead of cancellation."],"recommendation":"Option A is the most common resolution in real-world order management systems. Introduce credit notes as a compensating mechanism. This preserves pricing lock-in (operations need), maintains invoice immutability in the ledger (finance need — the invoice is never deleted, only offset), and enables pre-shipment cancellation (product/customer need). However, this requires explicit agreement from Finance that a credit note mechanism satisfies their 'irreversibility' constraint."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot define acceptance criteria — the requirements as stated are mutually exclusive.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is contradicted by the combination of BR-2 and BR-3. Implementing any subset without resolving the contradiction would silently violate at least one stakeholder's stated requirement."}]}
